title: The Pitch

jokes.text: Mr. Rose, you're stressing me out. And I just chugged a bottle of CBD oil.
jokes.setup: Johnny is anxiously stressing about being late for the pitch meeting.
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals she's already so anxious she drank an entire bottle of CBD oil — implying the stress-relief measure was itself an act of desperation.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie voice — deadpan, self-aware, and oddly specific. 'Chugged a bottle' escalates the anxiety to an absurd degree. Character-specific and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm going to be late. This is exactly the kind of thing that makes me look unprofessional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, given our track record, being laughed out of the room is probably accurate.

jokes.text: There is a jet? Can I come? / I already asked.
jokes.setup: Johnny mentions a jet has been sent for the pitch meeting.
jokes.punchline: David immediately wants to come; Alexis has already asked and been denied — implying both adult children are equally immature about the business trip.
jokes.explanation: The rapid two-beat exchange is economically tight. 'I already asked' is the better punchline — it confirms Alexis's entitlement and David's redundancy in one breath. Feels very true to both characters.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm stressed.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I've already consumed an entire bottle of CBD oil.

jokes.text: I'll swap out with someone.
jokes.setup: David has just been told he can't come on the jet.
jokes.punchline: Rather than accept it, David immediately proposes swapping out one of the legitimate business participants — revealing his total lack of awareness about his usefulness.
jokes.explanation: Three words that perfectly encapsulate David's self-importance. Maximum economy. The absurdity of him suggesting he replace a business partner is pure character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need your help with something.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't help you. But I can offer you my support.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Those are the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, help is doing something. Support is just being here while you do it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So you're refusing to help me, but you're willing to stand nearby?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'm very supportive that way.

jokes.text: Make sure they feed him lots of booze on the flight.
jokes.setup: The family is saying farewell to Johnny before his big pitch trip.
jokes.punchline: Moira's parting advice for her husband before a major business pitch is to get him drunk — a darkly practical but completely inappropriate suggestion.
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — the advice is simultaneously caring and oblivious. It's an understated joke that works because of character consistency rather than any structural cleverness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, there's a private jet?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I already tried to get on it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I will trade you places if you want.

jokes.text: Johnny, woo! Look at me. I'm you. / I can't believe your suit fits me, we're completely different sizes. And I have a much more athletic build.
jokes.setup: Roland is wearing Johnny's suit for the pitch meeting.
jokes.punchline: Roland brags about his 'much more athletic build' while acknowledging their suits fit interchangeably — a completely delusional self-assessment.
jokes.explanation: Roland's cheerful delusion about his physique is a classic Roland beat. 'I'm you' as an opening is charming before the illogic of claiming different sizes while sharing a suit lands perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This suit fits me perfectly. I have a much more athletic build than Johnny.

jokes.text: He looks the same age as the day I met him. / Oh, I'm sure he's just as handsome as he's ever been.
jokes.setup: Moira watches Roland wearing Johnny's suit.
jokes.punchline: Alexis, not looking, gives a generic compliment — clearly referring to Johnny, not Roland. The disconnect between what Moira sees (Roland) and what Alexis assumes (Johnny) creates the joke.
jokes.explanation: A quiet misdirection joke — Moira means Roland while Alexis means Johnny. Works as a gentle visual-context-dependent gag. Relies on the audience understanding who's speaking about whom.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland, you're looking... well.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, that's so nice of you to say that.

jokes.text: Rollie, there's mints in your pocket. There was a lot of onions in the omelette.
jokes.setup: Moira is delivering a grand farewell to the business team.
jokes.punchline: Mid-encouragement, Moira pivots to a very practical note about Roland's breath — undercutting the grandeur with mundane domesticity.
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash from grand to mundane is perfectly Moira. The specificity of 'a lot of onions in the omelette' gives it texture. She addresses it to Roland mid-pep-talk without breaking stride.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland, before we go in there, we need to talk about your breath. It's absolutely devastating. Did you have onions for lunch?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What? No, I didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, something died in your mouth, and I'm not going in that meeting until you've dealt with it.

jokes.text: Goodbye, my conquistadors! We are all relying on you... No! We're cheering you on!
jokes.setup: Moira delivers a theatrical farewell to Johnny and Roland.
jokes.punchline: She starts with 'we are all relying on you' — too honest, too much pressure — and immediately self-corrects with false cheerfulness.
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional character joke. The self-correction reveals Moira's anxiety beneath the theatrical performance — she accidentally says the truth, catches herself, and pivots. 'Conquistadors' is quintessential Moira vocabulary. The structure of accidental honesty → overcorrection is perfectly executed and very quotable. Justifies 8+ score: it's character-specific, structurally elegant, and emotionally resonant.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to miss you all so very much. You've become like family to me, and I expect you to make me proud, to reach for the stars, to achieve greatness, to—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: —or, you know, just be happy. That's fine too. Whatever works for you.

jokes.text: Can we maybe stop with the drum circle? Just — I'm just not feeling great. Are private planes always that bumpy?
jokes.setup: Roland is enthusiastically doing some kind of celebratory drumming on the plane.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's hangover-adjacent complaint about the private jet being 'bumpy' — implying she's never been on one and her nerves/CBD oil combo is not sitting well.
jokes.explanation: Funny callback energy to the CBD oil joke — Stevie's composure is already crumbling before the meeting. 'Drum circle' is a funny exaggeration for whatever Roland was doing. Solid character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I can't do this anymore. The constant swaying, the noise, the smell of diesel fuel mixed with someone's cologne that costs more than my car.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm motion sick on a yacht. A YACHT, David. This is supposed to be a luxury experience and I'm green like I'm on a fishing boat in a storm.

jokes.text: Uh, do you know if there is water where we're going? 'Cause I just got a sudden case of dry mouth.
jokes.setup: They've just arrived at the corporate offices and are being greeted professionally.
jokes.punchline: Stevie interrupts the formal greeting to ask about water due to 'dry mouth' — her nervousness manifesting physically in the most awkward possible moment.
jokes.explanation: The dry mouth is both a literal CBD side effect callback and a general anxiety symptom — double-layered. Her asking about water in the middle of a formal introduction is perfectly cringe. Escalating pattern of Stevie's composure disintegrating.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is going to be our best quarter yet, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Sure, yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I guess so.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland? Stevie? A little more enthusiasm, please?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yep.

jokes.text: I always loved the free popcorn. Nice touch.
jokes.setup: Ruth warmly recalls going to Rose Video as a child.
jokes.punchline: Her nostalgic memory of Johnny Rose's empire is... the free popcorn. Not the selection, the service, the ambiance — popcorn. Mildly deflating but sweetly so.
jokes.explanation: Gentle joke — the joke is that the thing she remembers about a major video empire is the complimentary snack. Works as a soft character beat for Ruth and a gentle deflation for Johnny's ego.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm getting a little anxious about the water situation. Like, my stomach's in knots, my palms are sweating, and I think I'm having heart palpitations.

jokes.text: Well, thank you, Ruth. It's always important to make the experience more personalized. / I couldn't agree more.
jokes.setup: Ruth has just complimented the free popcorn at Rose Video.
jokes.punchline: Johnny pivots the popcorn compliment into a business philosophy statement — 'personalized experience' — and Ruth earnestly agrees, as if this was wisdom.
jokes.explanation: Johnny smoothing over the mildly deflating memory with corporate-speak is very true to his character. The joke is in his slight desperation to find dignity in the popcorn compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: When will he be back?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: He's gone for a week.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I can wait.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: Not here, you can't.

jokes.text: Hey does anybody want a mint before we go up to the boardroom? I'm looking at you, Johnny.
jokes.setup: Moira told Roland to take mints because of the onion omelette.
jokes.punchline: Roland, having been the one told to take the mints, instead offers them to Johnny and calls him out — reversing the dynamic and perhaps projecting.
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that flips the dynamic — Roland was the one warned about onion breath, but he points the finger at Johnny. Classic Roland obliviousness or deflection. Economical and lands cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Ruth
dialogue.line: You know, I have such fond memories of Rose Apothecary. There was this nice touch you had.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Really? What was that?

dialogue.speaker: Ruth
dialogue.line: Free popcorn.

jokes.text: Moira packing 'as a precaution' — folding cherished items into luggage before the deal is even confirmed.
jokes.setup: Alexis walks in to find Moira doing something suspicious.
jokes.punchline: Moira has already begun packing to move to New York before the pitch has even been delivered, framing it as 'light reorganization' with a 'precaution' excuse.
jokes.explanation: The visual of Moira already packed before a hypothetical deal is pure character — her inability to suppress anticipation dressed up in denial language. 'Neatly folded into a piece of luggage' is wonderfully euphemistic Moira-speak.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: This is a really nice... I mean, it's just an office.

jokes.text: God forbid I find myself once again being forcefully evacuated and given no time to do a thorough sweep.
jokes.setup: Alexis catches Moira packing prematurely and Moira must explain herself.
jokes.punchline: Moira refers to their expulsion from their wealthy life as being 'forcefully evacuated' — dramatic, self-victimizing language that also inadvertently indicts their past lifestyle.
jokes.explanation: Moira's word choice is impeccable — 'forcefully evacuated' for losing their fortune is grandiose victim language that's also weirdly apt. The callback to their past trauma wrapped in theatrical vocabulary is quintessentially Moira and gets richer on rewatch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I am not packing. I am simply organizing my wardrobe into a series of carefully curated, travel-ready ensembles. Each outfit has been thoughtfully composed to maximize versatility while maintaining my signature aesthetic. I've color-coordinated by season, fabric weight, and occasion. The steamer trunks are merely a storage solution—a temporary one, I might add—and the fact that they're currently stationed by the door is purely coincidental. So no, I am absolutely not packing.

jokes.text: Alexis, let's not count our poultry before it's incubated.
jokes.setup: Alexis is upset about possibly moving to New York.
jokes.punchline: Moira mangles 'don't count your chickens before they hatch' into 'count our poultry before it's incubated' — same meaning, far more Moira.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Moira's malapropistic idiom is a signature comedic device for the show, and this is an excellent specimen. 'Poultry' for 'chickens' and 'incubated' for 'hatched' are the exact right level of adjacent-but-wrong. It's highly quotable, perfectly character-specific, and the logic is just coherent enough to land. Justifies 8+: it's creative, specific to Moira, and genuinely funny wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I learned my lesson the last time. One moment you're living in a penthouse, the next you're living in a barn. So I keep a bag packed at all times, just in case lightning strikes twice.

jokes.text: depending on what we acquire, we might not even have a guest room available.
jokes.setup: Alexis is upset that Moira might move without telling her.
jokes.punchline: Moira's follow-up concern isn't about Alexis's feelings — it's whether they'll have room for her in the new place. A casually cruel non-comfort.
jokes.explanation: Moira's maternal instinct expressed as real-estate logistics. The warmth-disguised-as-practicality is a classic Moira move. Gets a laugh from the understated dismissal of Alexis's emotional concern.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: One mustn't prematurely enumerate one's poultry prior to the emergence of said fowl from their calcium-based encasements.

jokes.text: You would have no way of knowing this, but usually when someone sends a jet for you, it means he's very interested. Trust me. And Tiger Woods.
jokes.setup: David asks if the deal is likely to go through.
jokes.punchline: Alexis cites herself and Tiger Woods as her data points for jet-sending etiquette — a completely absurd celebrity name-drop that implies a scandalous shared experience.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. 'And Tiger Woods' is perfectly dropped — it implies a shared jet experience that raises infinite questions. The combination of sincere authority ('trust me') and absurd credential is quintessential Alexis. The economy of the Tiger Woods addition is surgical. Justifies 8+: original, quotable, character-specific, earns the laugh through genuine wit.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're all moving? And nobody thought to include me in this conversation?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, we were going to tell you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: When? After you'd already left? I'm literally standing right here.

jokes.text: I knew you'd get all panic-y and weird about this, so I'm looking for a two bedroom. You're welcome.
jokes.setup: David is surprised and upset about possibly moving to New York.
jokes.punchline: Alexis pre-emptively included David in her apartment search — but presents it as a magnanimous gift requiring gratitude, not consultation.
jokes.explanation: 'You're welcome' before anyone has thanked her is classic Alexis entitlement. She's done something considerate in the most presumptuous way possible. The 'panic-y and weird' description of David is also accurate and cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I've been on so many private jets that I basically know everything about them. I've flown with Tiger Woods, I've flown with... well, mostly Tiger Woods.

jokes.text: Oh, David, you try finding an affordable one-bedroom in a place that's not Atlantic City.
jokes.setup: David objects to sharing an apartment with Alexis, pointing out he's married.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's rebuttal is that the only affordable alternative to sharing with her is Atlantic City — a very specific and low-status destination that implies she's done the research.
jokes.explanation: Atlantic City is the perfect specific choice — it's associated with desperation, seediness, and compromise. The joke implies Alexis has actually looked and this is her finding. Very quotable and very true to both the character and the NYC housing market.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're really going to move in with Patrick?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I've lived in twelve different countries, David. I know all the cheap housing markets. Budapest's great right now, but honestly, the best deal is definitely a studio apartment above a depanneur in Montreal.

jokes.text: This is a lot of information to process on a Tuesday morning.
jokes.setup: David has just been told his entire family is possibly moving to New York.
jokes.punchline: His response focuses on the day of the week — 'a Tuesday morning' — as if the timing is the primary issue, not the life upheaval.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'a Tuesday morning' as the complaint is perfectly David — prioritizing the wrong thing. It's a small, economy joke that lands through understatement.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And look at this Murphy bed! It's like having two rooms in one. During the day it's a spacious living area, and at night it transforms into a bedroom. It's basically architecture for people who can't commit to anything.

jokes.text: Oh my God, David, look at this one. It overlooks a park. I would have my own bathroom. And oh my God, your bedroom would have one of those little beds that comes down from the wall.
jokes.setup: David is trying to process the news; Alexis is enthusiastically apartment hunting.
jokes.punchline: The apartment she's found has great features for her — park view, own bathroom — but David and Patrick would get a Murphy bed, treated as an equally exciting perk.
jokes.explanation: The escalating selfishness — she gets the good stuff, he gets a wall-bed — dressed up in Alexis's signature enthusiasm is very funny. 'Those little beds that comes down from the wall' as a feature pitched to a married couple is delightfully tone-deaf.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, hello. I know this is awkward.

jokes.text: Okay, we're two people. We would get the master bedroom. / But I have a lot of coats.
jokes.setup: David asserts he and Patrick deserve the master bedroom as a married couple.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's counterargument is her coat collection — treated as equivalent to a marriage.
jokes.explanation: Five words that perfectly encapsulate Alexis. Her material possessions genuinely rival — in her mind — David's marital status as a claim to the master bedroom. The economy is near-perfect: 'But I have a lot of coats' needs no elaboration. Justifies 8+: it's one of the funniest lines in the episode, highly quotable, and entirely character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Bryce
dialogue.line: So, how was your flight?

dialogue.speaker: Tripp
dialogue.line: Oh, you know, smooth sailing. First class all the way.

dialogue.speaker: Bryce
dialogue.line: Must be nice. I heard Johnny Rose had to take the bus here. Well, I assume he did—I'm not sure he can afford first class anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Tripp
dialogue.line: Oh man, that's rough. How far the mighty have fallen, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Bryce
dialogue.line: I know, right? I actually feel bad for the guy. One minute you're a mogul, the next you're...

dialogue.speaker: Tripp
dialogue.line: Completely broke? Living in a small town? Trying to figure out what a Schitt's Creek even is?

jokes.text: I have been looking forward to seeing Kerry Washington in that all-female revival of '12 Angry Men.'
jokes.setup: David begins processing what moving to New York would mean for him.
jokes.punchline: His New York ambition is a very specific, very David cultural event — a gender-swapped prestige theatre production, cited with complete seriousness.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Kerry Washington in an all-female revival of '12 Angry Men' is the perfect David cultural touchstone — high-brow, progressive, niche. It signals his entire personality in one sentence.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate the feedback. You know, when you've spent as much time in the hospitality industry as I have, you learn to take criticism with a certain... grace. It's all part of the service.

jokes.text: Okay, David, I'm gonna have to bring you down to earth for a sec. There is no way you're getting tickets for that show. But I love your enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: David excitedly mentions the Kerry Washington show.
jokes.punchline: Alexis deflates his cultural ambition not on artistic grounds but on logistics — tickets — while patronizingly praising his 'enthusiasm.'
jokes.explanation: Alexis being the realistic one while patronizing David is a funny role reversal. 'But I love your enthusiasm' is condescending in exactly the right Alexis way. Also implies Alexis could probably get the tickets.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: And that's why this business model is going to revolutionize the industry because, you know, people want things and we're going to give them those things.

dialogue.speaker: Bryce
dialogue.line: Wow, so you're saying people want things? That's groundbreaking.

jokes.text: I will be putting our names down for that ticket lottery to see Kerry because Elle Fanning is supposed to be incredible as the foreman.
jokes.setup: Alexis told David he can't get tickets; he said he'd talk to Patrick.
jokes.punchline: On his way out, David reveals he's already pivoted from 'Kerry Washington' to 'Elle Fanning as the foreman' — escalating the cultural specificity and treating the lottery as his genuine plan.
jokes.explanation: The callback escalation to 'Elle Fanning as the foreman' is brilliant — it adds a new celebrity and a specific role, making the imaginary show more detailed and more absurd. 'The foreman' as a role in '12 Angry Men' given extra weight by David is perfect. Alexis's scandalized 'Ah-ah-ah-ah!' response (without text) implies the show is even more coveted than David lets on. Justifies 8+: structurally excellent callback, deeply character-specific, highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So... does anyone know what 'synergy' means?

jokes.text: Well, good afternoon, everyone. I'd like to say, long time no see, but Mike's not here and we've just just met.
jokes.setup: Johnny opens the pitch meeting after the awkward revelation that Mike isn't present.
jokes.punchline: He tries to open with a warm pleasantry, then immediately dismantles it by acknowledging Mike's absence and that he doesn't know anyone in the room.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's attempt at warmth undermined by honesty is endearing and mildly funny. The self-aware deflation of the opener is charming rather than hilarious — solid professional comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm just going to say it. This whole thing has me absolutely cacking up!

jokes.text: Hope the jet was Johnny Rose approved. / Although I guess at this point, coach would be Johnny Rose approved?
jokes.setup: Bryce welcomes the team and asks about the jet.
jokes.punchline: He immediately implies that Johnny's fortunes have fallen so far that even economy class would satisfy him now — a casual, slightly cruel jab at his reduced circumstances.
jokes.explanation: The joke works as a social cruelty — Bryce is a bit of a jerk and this establishes that. The 'coach would be approved' line is punchy. Slightly diminished by not being character-specific enough (any wealthy-turned-poor person joke).

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Do you have any allergies I should know about?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, I don't think so.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Great, I'll get you some water.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God!

jokes.text: Quite the tumble you took there, Johnny.
jokes.setup: Bryce continues referencing Johnny's fall from wealth.
jokes.punchline: The understatement of calling losing an empire a 'tumble' while being cheerfully casual about it is darkly funny.
jokes.explanation: 'Quite the tumble' for losing everything is a very good understatement. Bryce is positioned as slightly villainous — his cheerful put-downs of Johnny set up Roland's later defense of him.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What is all this?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: We're throwing you a surprise party!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, how delightful! You little gawpuses!

jokes.text: Could have happened to any one of us.
jokes.setup: Bryce has made a jab about Johnny's fall from wealth.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's response is gracious but the line is darkly funny — because no, a catastrophic financial collapse doesn't happen to just anyone. His dignified non-reaction IS the joke.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's gracious deflection of the put-down with a flat, absurd counter is perfect. 'Could have happened to any one of us' about losing a video empire is comedically wrong in a very understated way. Shows his dignity under fire.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was in a meeting with some very important corporate cognizant, and I realized I had no idea what they were talking about.

jokes.text: I should hope so. It's why we're all here.
jokes.setup: Stevie says 'that's a good question' after Bryce fills in 'invest' for her.
jokes.punchline: Bryce dryly confirms that yes, they're there to answer that question — a flat, slightly withering response to her fumbled recovery.
jokes.explanation: A well-placed dry quip that adds to Bryce's slightly antagonistic persona. Economy is excellent but it's not especially original.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've always called New York the Big Red Delicious. Much more accurate.

jokes.text: Should I just wait until it unfreezes?
jokes.setup: The presentation has frozen on 'Reinvigoration.'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's response to a tech failure in front of investors is to ask if she should just... wait. Her helplessness is both sympathetic and cringe-worthy.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's helpless question is funny because of how resigned it is — she's not problem-solving, she's just asking if she should stand there. Very true to Stevie in an unfamiliar environment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You timorous grinagog.

jokes.text: Billion dollar company and none of us can stop technology from cacking out, am I right?
jokes.setup: The presentation has frozen in a boardroom full of corporate investors.
jokes.punchline: Roland breaks the silence with 'cacking out' — a very non-corporate term — and tries to bond with the investors over universal tech failure. 'Am I right?' is wonderfully misplaced.
jokes.explanation: 'Cacking out' in a boardroom setting is perfectly Roland — he's trying to be relatable in the most ungainly way possible. The 'am I right?' at the end of his nervous filler is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Grace
dialogue.line: Roland sent me an emoji of a head exploding.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, that's wonderful! He must be having such a good time!

dialogue.speaker: Grace
dialogue.line: Moira, that emoji means he's very angry.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I prefer to think he's just very excited about life.

jokes.text: What are you guys pumpin' in here, pollen? Do you mind if I have some of that water there, please.
jokes.setup: The tech has been called to fix the presentation; there's a brief break.
jokes.punchline: Roland asks if they're pumping pollen into the room (implying allergies) and then reaches for someone else's water — a double social violation in a boardroom setting.
jokes.explanation: Two Roland violations stacked: blaming the air quality and helping himself to someone's water. The 'pollen' complaint is an endearingly absurd thing to say in a corporate setting. Very Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Everything is going to be fine. How do I know? Because I've decided it will be. And when have my decisions ever been wrong? Well, that's not the point. The point is, I have a very good feeling about this, and my feelings are never wrong. Well, almost never. But when they are wrong, it's usually because someone else made a decision that contradicted my feeling, which means it's not really my feeling that was wrong. You see? Everything will be fine.

jokes.text: Roland spills the water — 'Jesus.' / 'Oh gosh!'
jokes.setup: Roland has awkwardly reached for water in the boardroom.
jokes.punchline: He spills it — turning an already cringe social moment into physical chaos. The reactions ('Jesus,' 'Oh gosh!') suggest significant spillage.
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that's earned through escalation — Roland's violations have been building. The spill is predictable but executed at the right moment. Not especially original but effective in context.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I brought you a muffin.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I don't want a muffin.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a really good muffin.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, a muffin is not going to fix this.

jokes.text: Oh gals! You shouldn't have. Jocelyn, are we now moonlighting as town crier? Cheeky gawpus you.
jokes.setup: Moira arrives at what she assumes is a party celebrating her impending move to New York.
jokes.punchline: Her grand entrance language — 'moonlighting as town crier,' 'cheeky gawpus' — is hilariously over-the-top for a small-town gathering, and she's completely wrong about why they're celebrating.
jokes.explanation: 'Cheeky gawpus' is a vintage Moira obscure insult-term of endearment. Her theatrical assumptions about the party being for her, and the vocabulary she deploys, is peak Moira. Highly character-specific and rewatchable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This apartment has been featured in three different television shows.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you know it's good.

jokes.text: Hence our impending move to the Big Red Delicious.
jokes.setup: Moira is announcing their hypothetical move to New York.
jokes.punchline: She calls New York City 'the Big Red Delicious' — a malapropism mixing 'The Big Apple' (apple variety being Red Delicious) into a completely wrong phrase.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. 'The Big Red Delicious' is a perfect Moira malapropism — she clearly knows the nickname involves apples, gets the apple variety right (Red Delicious), but completely bungles the assembly. It's simultaneously wrong and logical, which is the hallmark of great malapropism comedy. Highly quotable, utterly character-specific, and lands with genuine surprise. Justifies 8+: it's inventive wordplay that only this character could deliver, and it rewards audiences who understand WHY it's wrong.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know what? I think you should eat something. It'll make you feel better.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, did you just... Did you just agree with me? Just like that? No resistance, no counter-argument about how you're 'fine' or how you 'don't need anything'? Patrick, are you okay? Should I be concerned? Are we breaking up?

jokes.text: Excuse me? / Moira, I haven't said anything yet. Why the festivities then?
jokes.setup: Moira announces her move to 'the Big Red Delicious'; the room is confused.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn points out she hasn't said anything yet — the celebration Moira thought was for her departure is actually for something else entirely.
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection — Moira has imposed her own narrative on a gathering that has nothing to do with her. The revelation that she got it completely wrong compounds her premature celebration joke nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A motel is a place where people stay when they're traveling and they need somewhere to rest their head for the night. It's like a hotel, but more... accessible.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I mean, not that there's anything wrong with that. Some very nice people stay in motels. I'm sure you've stayed in plenty of motels. Do you stay in motels? You probably do.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What I'm trying to say is, it's a perfectly respectable establishment.

jokes.text: Grace is moving to Elm Valley. That's why the cake says 'Goodbye Grace.'
jokes.setup: Moira assumed the party and cake were for her departure to New York.
jokes.punchline: The cake literally says 'Goodbye Grace' — for a completely different person's departure. Moira's entire entrance was misplaced.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the cake's message landing as the payoff is effective. Simple but clean misdirection structure. Moira's grandiosity deflated by a grocery store cake.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I actually love living in a motel. It's convenient, it's clean, and the staff is always there if you need anything.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Of course, I didn't choose this lifestyle — I'm forced to live here because my family lost everything — but that's beside the point.

jokes.text: Oh I... I thought you were singling out one of my notable attributes.
jokes.setup: Moira is told the cake says 'Goodbye Grace' for a different person.
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals she thought 'Grace' on the cake was a compliment to her — as if she possesses the quality of grace and someone baked it onto a cake in her honour.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. The logic that 'Goodbye Grace' could be a tribute to Moira's personal grace is so perfectly Moira — narcissistic, theatrical, and internally consistent with her character. The audience can picture exactly the headspace she was in reading that cake. Pure character comedy at its best. Justifies 8+: it's original, structurally perfect, and could only be Moira Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That was a great presentation, Johnny. Really professional. I gotta say, I came in here thinking you were gonna absolutely bomb it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, thank you, Roland. I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you didn't. You were great. Say, uh, you didn't happen to see a briefcase around here, did you? Brown leather?

jokes.text: Shh. Okay, we'll just smooth things over until Grace arrives. / I-I'm right here. / Yes you are! Grace! Our timorous grinagog. I think I'll miss you most of all.
jokes.setup: Moira's faux pas is exposed; she needs to cover.
jokes.punchline: Moira dramatically pivots to showering Grace with affection and a bizarre Moira-ism ('timorous grinagog') while pretending she knew Grace was there all along.
jokes.explanation: 'Timorous grinagog' is another vintage Moira vocabulary word — likely obscure or made-up — deployed to cover her social error with theatrical warmth. 'I think I'll miss you most of all' directed at someone she clearly doesn't know at all is wonderfully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What was even in that briefcase?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Nothing. Absolutely nothing. I just carried it around to look important.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you've been walking around with an empty briefcase this whole time?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's right. It's all about the perception, Stevie. People see a briefcase, they think you've got it all figured out.

jokes.text: You've received correspondence? What am I looking at? A cartoon head exploding? / Oh. It looks like three cartoon heads exploding. / Well, then they're blowing people's minds. What's the concern?
jokes.setup: Jocelyn got an ambiguous emoji text from Roland during the pitch.
jokes.punchline: Moira insists the 'heads exploding' emoji means they're 'blowing people's minds' — a willfully optimistic misreading driven by her refusal to acknowledge failure.
jokes.explanation: Moira's spin on the exploding head emoji is great character comedy — her denial reflex converts a distress signal into a triumph. Jocelyn's literal description ('three cartoon heads exploding') vs. Moira's optimistic reading is a nice contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Bryce
dialogue.line: You're trying to build a business in industries that are essentially dead. It's like trying to start a buggy whip company in 2019.

jokes.text: The deal has to happen. Because it has to. Because there is no alternative. Okay? It's as simple as that.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn and others are expressing concern about the pitch.
jokes.punchline: Moira's entire 'argument' is circular — it must happen because it must. Her grip on Jocelyn's wrist completes the picture of barely-contained panic.
jokes.explanation: The circular logic ('it has to happen because it has to') is funny as a rhetorical form. Paired with the wrist grip, it's a great reveal of Moira's actual emotional state beneath the performance.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know what? Johnny Rose is one of the most thoughtful, genuine people I know. He cares deeply about the people around him, he's funny, he's smart, and he's done nothing but try to help everyone in this town. So maybe you could show him some respect.

jokes.text: You're gripping my wrist very tightly, Moira.
jokes.setup: Moira has been insisting the deal will happen while visibly panicking.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's calm, flat observation that her wrist is being gripped very tightly is the perfect straight-man punctuation to Moira's speech.
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect — one sentence that deflates Moira's entire speech. Jocelyn's deadpan is the show's best straight-man work. The quiet physical detail (the wrist grip) being the punchline is elegant.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Johnny is a good man. He's kind, he's thoughtful, he's one of the most genuine people I know.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: And anyone who doesn't see that is—

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, sorry. Is Johnny in the room? Because I don't want him to think I'm getting soft on him.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: But yes. Everything I said. He's great.

jokes.text: Okay. Well, let's just continue celebrating Grace. / What? / I'm Grace. / I know. But she's not... you're — you're not celebrating enough. No.
jokes.setup: Moira needs to pivot from her anxiety meltdown.
jokes.punchline: Moira instructs Grace to celebrate 'more' — demanding enthusiasm from the guest of honour while Moira herself recovers composure. Her direction to someone at their own party is wonderfully imperious.
jokes.explanation: Moira directing a stranger to celebrate harder at their own party is a wonderful power move disguised as hospitality. 'You're not celebrating enough' as a diagnosis of someone else's party behaviour is peak Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's not an idiot, he's just Canadian.

jokes.text: I don't think you took long enough.
jokes.setup: David has been slow to return to the store and Patrick has been waiting.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's dry greeting — 'I don't think you took long enough' — is playful sarcasm that signals he knows something is coming.
jokes.explanation: Classic Patrick dry wit — economical and warm. Sets the tone for their conversation. Nothing groundbreaking but perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Dear Universe, I'm writing to report a missing person. Male, approximately six feet tall, perpetually unimpressed expression, answers to Johnny Rose. Last seen wearing something from the clearance rack at a department store. If spotted, please return immediately. He's been missing his sense of humor for approximately thirty years.

jokes.text: Okay, am I holding a treat for you? / I don't know, are you?
jokes.setup: Patrick asks what took David so long.
jokes.punchline: The 'treat' dynamic — David implying he has news and Patrick playing along as if David is a dog returning with something — is a sweet, playful beat in their relationship.
jokes.explanation: The 'treat' metaphor is sweetly absurd — David packages news as if he's a dog that found something. Patrick's 'I don't know, are you?' is the right warm-playful response. Characterful and economic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Dear God, I come before you in this moment of profound vulnerability, seeking your divine guidance and—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, can I use your hand cream? Mine are like, so dry right now.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, I am in the middle of a spiritual communion.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but seriously, look at this. It's like the Sahara.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Have you considered a hydrating mask? There's a wonderful Icelandic one that—actually, those cuticles are concerning. Yes, use the hand cream.

jokes.text: One of them was featured in season 3 of 'Sex and the City.'
jokes.setup: David tells Patrick that Alexis has been researching New York apartments.
jokes.punchline: David's citation of 'Sex and the City' as a real-estate credential is perfectly him — cultural cachet is a valid apartment metric in David's world.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'season 3' (not just 'Sex and the City') is very David — he knows the show well enough to cite seasons. It's a legitimately funny character detail that says everything about how he filters information.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you won't take the position, perhaps you'd consider a role in our new division. We're expanding into... let's call it 'lifestyle curation.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Of course, I should mention that I've been embezzling from the company for three years, and I'm doing it right now, in this office, while wearing Valentino.

jokes.text: So there's a good chance that the deal won't go through. In which case, you know, this was just a big waste of time and frown lines.
jokes.setup: David is trying to reassure Patrick that the New York move is speculative.
jokes.punchline: His summary of worst-case scenario ends with 'frown lines' — as if the cosmetic consequence of worrying is the real loss.
jokes.explanation: 'Frown lines' as the tragedy of a failed business deal is very David — the physical cost of stress prioritized. Charming and specific.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It's like we're waiting for the results of a game show, but instead of winning a car, we're winning our lives back.

jokes.text: I thought you'd be excited. Would half a muffin help? / I'm not sure. / Okay. Y-you took it so that's a good sign.
jokes.setup: Patrick is thrown by the news of a potential move; David tries to manage him.
jokes.punchline: The muffin as an emotional tool — offered, accepted with uncertainty, then the acceptance read as positive progress — is a very specific, very sweet David-Patrick dynamic.
jokes.explanation: The muffin-as-barometer bit is genuinely charming and funny. 'You took it so that's a good sign' is David reading Patrick's emotional state through pastry consumption. Warm, specific, and economical.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, you've been doing an amazing job at the motel.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Thank you, David.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, she had a good teacher. I showed her everything she knows about running that place.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Roland, you don't even work there.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Not officially.

jokes.text: Okay. Y-you took it so that's a good sign.
jokes.setup: David has offered Patrick half his muffin and Patrick has taken it silently.
jokes.punchline: David interprets the muffin acceptance as a positive emotional signal — reading Patrick's feelings through his muffin behavior.
jokes.explanation: David parsing Patrick's emotional state through muffin acceptance is a tiny, perfect joke. The logic ('you took it, good sign') is both absurd and emotionally accurate — it IS a good sign. The joke works on character and story levels simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We only have this cheap champagne substitute.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, David, don't be such a snob. This is perfectly adequate. In fact, I find it rather delightful. It's like they took all the pretension out of champagne and left only the joy.

jokes.text: So I came in two under par and that is with half the course under water... it ruined the whole trip.
jokes.setup: The tech has been fixed; the team returns to the boardroom where Bryce is mid-story.
jokes.punchline: Bryce is complaining that a flooded golf course 'ruined the whole trip' — oblivious to or unbothered by the actual purpose of the meeting, establishing his privileged irrelevance.
jokes.explanation: Functional character-establishing joke for Bryce — he's wealthy, out of touch, and has been telling golf stories. The 'ruined the whole trip' punchline is mild. Necessary for context but not especially funny.

jokes.text: Now let me ask you, how many of you have stayed in a motel? / [pause] / Well, motels are small hotels without the golf courses in case you... Well, good-good-good. So you're familiar at least.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks the investors how many have stayed in a motel.
jokes.punchline: He begins explaining what a motel is (to billionaires who presumably have golf villas), then realizes some have actually stayed in one. His flustered recovery ('good-good-good') is charming.
jokes.explanation: The prepared 'small hotels without golf courses' definition getting interrupted by the answer is a nice reversal. Johnny's genuine surprise and quick pivot ('good-good-good') is natural and funny.

jokes.text: And that's what the Rosebud Motel Group plans to do. Open that window and revitalize the classic roadside motel for a new generation. Turns out, a couple of coats of paint and dusting off some mid-century furniture can go a long way.
jokes.setup: Johnny has built up the pitch with lofty language about windows into small-town life.
jokes.punchline: The grand plan reduces to: some paint and old furniture. The gap between the rhetorical buildup and the mundane reality is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The self-aware bathos of the pitch — Johnny's plan reduced to 'paint and old furniture' — is both funny and charming. Works within the scene's emotional arc.

jokes.text: We've seen a 400% increase in occupancy since we started marketing. And cleaning.
jokes.setup: Johnny is describing the motel's transformation and success.
jokes.punchline: Stevie adds 'and cleaning' as a dry afterthought — implying the motel's previous state was genuinely squalid and that hygiene was an afterthought in the business plan.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes — two words that completely undercut the professional pitch context and reveal the true history of the motel. 'And cleaning' as an addendum to a 400% occupancy increase is brutally funny in its honesty. Near-perfect economy. Stevie's deadpan is the exactly right delivery mode. Justifies 8+: it's original, structurally brilliant (the pause before it is the whole joke), and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Mr. Rose, you were so good. / Johnny, even I was impressed, and I thought you had blown it for all of us.
jokes.setup: Johnny has just delivered a moving pitch.
jokes.punchline: Roland's compliment includes the admission that he 'thought you had blown it for all of us' — a backhanded compliment that inadvertently reveals his low expectations.
jokes.explanation: Roland's compliment structure is classic Roland — the praise contains the insult. 'I thought you had blown it for all of us' as the baseline expectation he's working from is very him.

jokes.text: Didn't you have a briefcase? / See, this is what happens when Johnny impresses me. I get confused and I forget what I'm doing. / What was in that briefcase anyway? / Nothing. You told me to look intimidating. So I brought a briefcase.
jokes.setup: Post-pitch glow; the team is leaving the boardroom.
jokes.punchline: The briefcase was always empty — Johnny brought it purely as a prop for intimidation on Moira's instruction. The reveal that it contains nothing is a perfect punctuation joke.
jokes.explanation: The empty briefcase reveal is excellent — it recontextualizes the whole pitch scene (Johnny was literally carrying nothing) while perfectly reflecting Moira's theatrical approach to life. 'You told me to look intimidating' is a great line. Justifies 8+: it's a callback that lands harder than the original setup, character-specific, and very quotable.

jokes.text: See this is what happens when Johnny impresses me. I get confused and I forget what I'm doing.
jokes.setup: Roland has asked about the briefcase, and someone points out Roland had it.
jokes.punchline: Roland explains his confusion as a physiological response to being impressed by Johnny — a wonderfully specific and absurd piece of Roland self-analysis.
jokes.explanation: Roland explaining his cognitive failure as a direct result of Johnny's excellence is both a genuine compliment and absurdly self-aware. The specificity ('when Johnny impresses me') implies this is a pattern. High character integration.

jokes.text: Bryce snidely asking if Johnny wants to 'reinvent the pager next' — 'First a video store, now a motel chain. What? He wants to reinvent the pager next?'
jokes.setup: Johnny and the team have just left the room; Bryce immediately dismisses them.
jokes.punchline: Bryce mocks Johnny's business instincts as backward-looking — video stores, motels, pagers. The audience knows the pitch was actually good, so Bryce becomes the villain here.
jokes.explanation: The pager joke is functional — establishes Bryce as the antagonist and the industries he lists (video, motel, pager) do form a nice escalating list of 'dead' tech. But not especially original or character-specific.

jokes.text: I, uh... forgot my briefcase. Don't wanna forget that.
jokes.setup: Roland has overheard Bryce's dismissive comments about Johnny.
jokes.punchline: Roland's entrance to retrieve the briefcase — and his flat, mundane cover line — sets up his confrontation. The understatement of 'don't wanna forget that' while clearly seething is very funny.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the briefcase (which we now know is empty) is layered — Roland is retrieving a prop while hearing real insults. His cover story about not wanting to forget it is funny because we know there's nothing in it.

jokes.text: Do you people know what it took for Johnny Rose to come back and do this? After being away for all that time? Shame on you. Shame on all of you.
jokes.setup: Roland overheard Bryce dismissing Johnny; he returns to confront them.
jokes.punchline: Roland's passionate defense of Johnny — 'Shame on you. Shame on all of you.' — is unexpectedly moving and funny simultaneously. The repetition of 'shame' and the grandstanding from Roland is deeply character-based.
jokes.explanation: This joke works on multiple levels — it's funny because Roland is the last person you'd expect to be a dignified defender, yet his sincerity is genuine. 'Shame on all of you' is quotable and emotionally resonant. The character has evolved enough that this feels earned. Justifies 8+: it's a comedic beat that also has emotional weight, which is what Schitt's Creek does best.

jokes.text: Okay. I just realized you guys are flying us home. So I apologize for my outburst, but I don't regret it.
jokes.setup: Roland has just told the investors 'shame on all of you.'
jokes.punchline: He immediately walks back the confrontation — not because he's wrong, but because he needs a ride home. The mercenary motivation undercuts the nobility while making the apology funnier.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and possibly one of the best in the series. The structure is perfect: grand moral stand → immediate practical consideration → partial apology that maintains pride ('I don't regret it'). It works as pure comedy AND as character revelation. 'I apologize for my outburst, but I don't regret it' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. Justifies 8+: it's structurally flawless, completely character-specific, and lands at the peak of an emotional arc.

jokes.text: The man's a legend.
jokes.setup: Roland has just apologized and walked back to rejoin the team.
jokes.punchline: As a final parting shot, after the apology, Roland throws in 'The man's a legend' — reinstating his original position while technically complying with the apology.
jokes.explanation: Four words that are almost perfectly constructed. Roland has technically apologized but the exit line reinstates everything. The economy is exceptional — maximum character and humor per word. Justifies 8+: it's a callback that lands better than both its predecessors and does so in four words.

jokes.text: I don't believe the two of you were encouraging enough before he left. / I made him a card. / And I said 'take care.'
jokes.setup: Moira gathers the family, claiming they need to 'send energy' to Johnny.
jokes.punchline: David and Alexis defend their send-offs — a card and 'take care' — as if these are normal levels of support for a major business pitch. Both are hilariously inadequate.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between 'a card' (David, overachieving) and 'take care' (Alexis, minimal effort) as their respective contributions to Johnny's life-changing trip is very funny. Alexis's 'take care' is perfectly casual.

jokes.text: We need to do something. Send some energy his way. Hands, children. — What's going on? — Perhaps we should kneel.
jokes.setup: Moira decides the family must take collective action to help Johnny from afar.
jokes.punchline: Her proposed action is holding hands and sending 'energy' — and then escalates to suggesting they kneel, as if this is a séance or prayer ritual.
jokes.explanation: Moira's escalation from 'hold hands' to 'perhaps we should kneel' is a wonderful example of her theatrical instinct overwhelming practical judgment. Her improvised ritual is absurdist and very Moira.

jokes.text: Perhaps we should kneel.
jokes.setup: Moira has assembled the family to collectively send 'energy' to Johnny.
jokes.punchline: She escalates the ritual to kneeling — turning a group pep-talk into a quasi-religious ceremony.
jokes.explanation: One line that perfectly escalates the absurdity of Moira's ritual. The kneeling suggestion treats their concern like a religious observance, which is both funny and weirdly touching.

jokes.text: Ow! / Ooh.
jokes.setup: The family has been told to join hands for Moira's ritual.
jokes.punchline: Joining hands causes immediate minor physical pain — suggesting the family's lack of physical coordination or David's dramatic pain response.
jokes.explanation: Small physical beat — the discomfort of the family even touching each other is mildly funny. Not a strong joke in isolation but serves the scene rhythm.

jokes.text: Okay, are we praying? / Don't be ridiculous.
jokes.setup: The family is kneeling and holding hands in a circle.
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses the suggestion they're praying as 'ridiculous' — while conducting what is functionally a prayer ritual.
jokes.explanation: The denial is the joke — Moira's secular self-image conflicts with the religious structure of what she's doing. 'Don't be ridiculous' as a refusal to acknowledge the obvious is very Moira.

jokes.text: To Whom It May Concern: There's a man out there, my husband — he's out there trying to build back what we've lost. And... I'm sorry; 5'10', slow talker...
jokes.setup: Moira begins addressing 'To Whom It May Concern' on behalf of the family.
jokes.punchline: She starts describing Johnny as if filing a lost person report — '5'10'', slow talker' — as physical identifying characteristics for the universe.
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's finest jokes. 'To Whom It May Concern' as a prayer opener is perfect Moira — formal, theatrical, bureaucratic. But '5'10', slow talker' as the physical description she's submitting to the universe is genius. It's simultaneously a prayer, a missing persons report, and a love letter, filtered through Moira's particular brand of theatrical obliviousness. Justifies 8+: highly original, structurally surprising, and could only come from this character.

jokes.text: Okay. I think they get it.
jokes.setup: Moira is mid-description of Johnny to the universe.
jokes.punchline: David interrupts with 'I think they get it' — addressing a higher power's comprehension of the description as if it were a regular person who might be getting impatient.
jokes.explanation: David's impatient 'I think they get it' addressed to a metaphysical entity is very funny — he's treating the universe like a person who's been given enough information. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: He's worked so hard while the rest of us have just been flailing about.
jokes.setup: Moira is in the midst of her prayer/plea for Johnny.
jokes.punchline: She accidentally indicts herself and the children with 'flailing about' — a surprisingly honest self-assessment from someone who doesn't usually acknowledge her own uselessness.
jokes.explanation: The self-indictment of 'flailing about' from Moira is unexpectedly honest and funny. David and Alexis's immediate objections confirm they don't agree with the assessment.

jokes.text: I don't think that's accurate. / Absolutely not the case.
jokes.setup: Moira says the family has been 'flailing about' while Johnny worked.
jokes.punchline: Both children simultaneously object — confirming they do not accept the characterization, even as the audience can see it's fairly accurate.
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous objections from David and Alexis are a nice quick beat. Funny because both characters have objectively been directionless much of the series, making their objections funnier.

jokes.text: John. My macho man. You don't deserve another failure.
jokes.setup: Moira is speaking to the universe on Johnny's behalf.
jokes.punchline: She addresses Johnny as 'my macho man' — an incongruous, slightly absurd term of endearment — then immediately follows it with 'you don't deserve another failure,' which is a very backhanded form of prayer.
jokes.explanation: 'My macho man' as Moira's pet name for Johnny is both unexpected and somehow right. 'You don't deserve another failure' as a prayer/blessing implies a catalogue of previous failures, making it simultaneously supportive and darkly honest. Justifies 8+: it's a character joke that's also emotionally layered.

jokes.text: Oh, Alexis, your hands are as dry as an autumn leaf. / Eww. / Shh.
jokes.setup: The family is holding hands in their semi-prayer circle.
jokes.punchline: Moira interrupts the spiritual moment to comment on Alexis's hand texture — an extremely mundane observation delivered mid-ritual.
jokes.explanation: 'Dry as an autumn leaf' is Moira-level poetry deployed as a mundane observation. Breaking the ritual solemnity to critique Alexis's skincare is extremely on-brand and very funny.

jokes.text: I was in the room when they passed on Uber.
jokes.setup: Ruth is consoling the team about the rejection, saying the firm passes on good ideas.
jokes.punchline: She cites being present for the Uber pass as her credential — establishing both the firm's legendary bad judgment and her own insider status.
jokes.explanation: The Uber reference is well-chosen — it's a universally known 'pass' that validates both the motel idea and Ruth's rebellion. The casual delivery of 'I was in the room' is excellent understatement.

jokes.text: Okay, I hear another vehicle. Heavier tread.
jokes.setup: The family is waiting anxiously for the returning pitch team's car.
jokes.punchline: David has apparently been listening to the road so intently he can identify vehicles by their 'tread' — an absurdly specific automotive observation from a man who knows nothing about cars.
jokes.explanation: 'Heavier tread' from David is delightfully incongruous — he's not someone you'd expect to identify vehicles by sound. The anxiety of the wait has transformed him into an amateur tracker. Very funny specific detail.

jokes.text: Oh my God. They're gonna get here when they get here.
jokes.setup: David has just identified an approaching vehicle by tread.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's exasperated 'they're gonna get here when they get here' is perfectly ironic — she's the one who started hunting apartments before the deal was done.
jokes.explanation: The irony of Alexis — who was the most anticipatory — telling David to calm down is a nice character beat. Mild but effective.

jokes.text: This threat of failure is inhumane. Jocelyn: No, I think it's exciting. It's like 'Deal or No Deal', but with the rest of our lives.
jokes.setup: Moira is dramatically suffering through waiting for Johnny's return.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's cheerful analogy — 'Deal or No Deal but with the rest of our lives' — is an inadvertently horrifying reframe that Jocelyn presents as comforting.
jokes.explanation: 'Deal or No Deal but with the rest of our lives' is an excellent Jocelyn line — she means it as exciting and fun, but the actual content is existentially terrifying. The gap between her cheerful tone and the stakes she's describing is the joke. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+: original, well-structured contrast, and very character-specific to Jocelyn's oblivious optimism.

jokes.text: John! No!
jokes.setup: Johnny arrives home; Moira rushes to greet him.
jokes.punchline: Moira's initial reaction to seeing Johnny is 'John! No!' — implying she somehow initially misread his expression as bad news, or that she ran toward him and he can't handle the enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: The ambiguity of 'John! No!' is funny — it's unclear whether she's stopping herself, stopping him, or reacting to something physical. Her dramatic entrance energy peaks here before the good news arrives.

jokes.text: We only have Zampagne. / Zampagne for all!
jokes.setup: Johnny announces the deal and calls for champagne.
jokes.punchline: David corrects that they only have 'Zampagne' — presumably a cheap champagne substitute — and Johnny immediately rebrands the celebration around it without missing a beat.
jokes.explanation: 'Zampagne for all!' is an excellent tag on the episode-closing celebration. It perfectly encapsulates the show's premise — the Roses celebrate a major win with a budget substitute — and Johnny's immediate embrace of it is heartwarming and funny. Very quotable. Justifies the top end of its scores: it's a thematically resonant joke that closes the episode on the show's central theme.

jokes.text: I sealed the deal. It was really me.
jokes.setup: The team returns having secured a deal; everyone is celebrating.
jokes.punchline: Roland claims sole credit for the deal — after spilling water and giving a boardroom speech that required an immediate apology.
jokes.explanation: Roland claiming sole credit after a series of disasters is perfect — the audience knows exactly what he contributed (chaos and an apology) and his certainty about his role is completely delusional. Strong callback joke.

tmdb_overview: Johnny, Stevie and Roland head to New York for their pitch meeting, while the family anxiously awaits their return.
insight: Final season pivot trades laughs for heart; cringe comedy carries a lower-density sendoff.
